sentences.text: Well, the prostitute was really-- didn't give me food.
And not that I-- I was such a big eater, but I remember really going-- and she was quite punitive.
Because once she was putting an egg, and I tried to have a little bit of this egg.
And she punished me.
She put, I think, all salt, it was in the salt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] container on this egg, and told me for the punishment, you will eat that.
And I didn't see my mother often.
I always worried that she will be killed.
And one day, when the woman of the house was away, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody rang the door, and I had the feeling it's my mother.
So I was very, very quietly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lying down next to the door, and I heard my mother's voice-- 'Olenka.' So I opened the door.
And I told my mother that I don't want to be in this place.
That the woman is really bad for me.
And I also kind of felt bad about not having hair, like you know, [LAUGHS] being completely shaven.
And-- and my mother took me to another place.
At this time, my first name was Bozenka, and my last name was Goscinska.
And she took me to a place where there was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] man died.
He was a conductor of trains.
And he left three kids.
The oldest maybe-- they weren't kids, they were young adults.
One boy, maybe age of 18, and two girls, the oldest was maybe 25.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And they-- they really liked Bozenka, and I liked this people.
I pleased them.
That was the time when I had my communion as a Christian girl, praying to Jesus Christ.
And if somebody would tell me that I am Jewish, I would really be-- it would be terrible.
I was anti-Semitic.
OK?
[LAUGHS]
I was staying with this family.
They didn't have money.
My mother paid for-- for me.
And they were good, but it was terrible, because if somebody would find out that they keep me, I was hiding again.
Oh, [LAUGHS] between other places, they had two [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not hens-- two birds.
A hen house.
A chicken coop.
Yeah.
Who were fighting with them sometimes.
[LAUGHS] And they had a little closet which they kept them.
And when somebody was coming to their home, I was going to the closet that was closed, and frightened.

Once when German were around of the-- their house, they had-- I had to go on the street, because they were afraid if German will knock to their door and find me, the whole family will lose their life.
Money.
[LAUGHS]
I don't.
I don't.
But they really didn't have too much bread and too much food.
And they-- they were-- quite difficult for them to survive, because the people didn't work.
The-- the main guy who brought money was dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, their name was [INAUDIBLE], I even have a picture with them from this time, when I was staying with them, the family.
I was contacting them after the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother took me from them and brought me to the place where she was living.
And she was living in kind of-- under the building is a place where there are no windows.
You can see people's legs.
A cellar, kind of.
And that was terrible time, when my mother went out, and I was in the cellar alone, and-- because it was cold.
And it was hungry.
And I always worried that she will be killed, and I will be alone.
And sometimes to-- to make myself feel warm, there was-- I knew how to boil the water, how to make water warm.
And I was putting the water warm in the container and put my feet in it.
[LAUGHS] It was like in Andersen story about little match-- matches when she was trying to-- to make herself warmer, if you know the story.
Anyway, I was putting my feet into warm water, and that felt so good.
And very often, I-- I fell asleep.
And it was one of the worst feeling, when you wake up with your feet in cold water and no mother, and it's dark, and it's hungry.
And you worry, kind of, really.
And it was a lot of enjoyment when she came, [LAUGHS] you know, that she's still alive.
I wasn't an easy kid at the time, but my mother was always there for me.
And when it came to August '44, summer of '44, there was an uprising in Warsaw.
It was close to the end of the war.
There were no ghettos and there were no Jews, really.
They were all, you know, Polish people were fighting in Warsaw uprising in '44.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother-- it was a lot of bombing.
It was a lot of prayer.
The whole city was destroyed, really.
And at this time, nobody was really talking about the Jews so much.
Or I don't remember that-- that people were blackmailing me.
We were just taken with other Poles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know I was sick.
I know I had-- my nose was bleeding all the time.
And they took us with other Poles to some-- behind the Warsaw.
And so we didn't have any clothes but summer clothes.
It was summer.
No food, no-- no money.
Because it-- they just-- the building where we were started to burn.
So in '44, they took us to a very-- to a little village.
Very little village, where there were no food.
And when winter came, it was really horribly cold.
I didn't have shoes.
Do you believe that I was going on the snow with no shoes?
I had one dress, and there were a lot of lice in this dress.
OK?
I had no shoes.
I was really-- and I looked like a skeleton.
I didn't have food.
And my hair was growing.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother tried to work digging-- and I tried to work-- digging the potatoes from the ground.
But she was very sick.
She had tuberculosis pneumonia.
Like she was getting hemorrhages from her lungs.
And I worried a lot about her.
They were-- nobody was persecuting us at the time, that we were Jewish, it was just terrible-- terrible poverty and illness.
And I had these nightmares that my mother is dying.
And she-- she somehow could put me to some monastery with nuns and free herself from me for some time, until I escaped from monastery and came back to her.
I don't know.
Days, months?
Probably like one month.
But I think I really suffered a real depression there.
I had all these dreams that my mother is dying, and I just couldn't stand to be there because I was missing my mother.
So I escaped from this place, and I was-- it was sad that my mother wasn't happy to see me back, because she-- there was no food in place where she-- she stayed.
She was kind of dying.
Where did you find her?
In the same place when I left, which was like a-- a cottage and we were sleeping on the straw with some other people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then she finally put me to some place that was Polish orphanage home.
That was-- they were children who left Warsaw before Warsaw uprising.
Polish children who went to school or whatever.
And the parents didn't come.
They didn't know what happened to their parents.
And they didn't have any food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother went there and said that she's my aunt, and she's sick.
Would they be able to take me.
Spoke with some woman, and the woman agreed to take me.
And I remember my mother, I saw her last time then, and she was sitting and crying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they put me on the bed.
They took the only clothes I had from me, and I couldn't get out from this bed to walk naked.
I was there day and night.
I didn't have anything to wear.
But-- my moth-- and so I was lying on the lowest bed, because they had like made of wood.
Bunks.
And seeing my mother crying and sick, I didn't do anything.
I didn't say anything.
I was unable to-- to tell Mom I love you, or-- I just pretend that I didn't see it, see that.
Then when my mother-- and of course everybody knew that that was my aunt.
And I remember that I had all this night dreams that she's sick, and I don't know what happens with her.
And nobody knew my name other than Bozenka [?
Goscinska. ?] And nobody knew who [?
I was. ?] And my mother, at the time, was [?
Yanina ?] [?
Zhaswavska. ?] And I have it documents when she was writing the last few words to my father as [?
Yanina ?] [?
Zhaswavska. ?] And she always mentioned about my looks and so on.
I-- I have documentation of these times.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember when Russian came to this little village.
And I must say, they were good for children.
They took these children from the little cottage, and they put them in some very beautiful house.
[LAUGHS] Really, that's true.
Yes, with other kids.
Well, I only remember that I saw, first time, mirrors.
And, I mean, from I don't know how long.
And I was surprised to see myself.
[LAUGHS] Before, in this orphanage home, when they gave me a dress, and we were going out, kids who were kind of showing me and saying, oh, look at her.
She's already dead.
She's a skeleton.
I was so skinny.
And they were teasing me.
And I was very hungry.
And I remember that some kids stole some sugar and milk and were making candies.
And the smell of this was so wonderful.
And I don't know.
I was very inhibited, because all these kids were sitting and eating these candies, and I was there lying on this, how you call it?
A bunk?
A bunk.
And I wanted some so much, and I was not able to say, hey, you forgot me.
[LAUGHS] I don't know.
I remember-- you remember things.
Because I always think you have kind of sometimes be demanding or say what's on your mind.
But I was a kid who-- who looked at her mother crying and felt that-- would like to say something and pretend I didn't see it.
I was the kid who never said that I know that my grandmother has committed suicide and how.
And I thought about my grandmother all the time, and looking at the sky and stars and thinking, there is my grandmother, one of the stars.
Or-- or whatever children can make these stories.
But I never was able to say that I-- I never talked about my grandmother.
And I-- I remember I was not able to ask for a piece of candy, which smelled so well.
Anyway.
So I got to this nice house with other kids.
I didn't know what happened with my mother.
And parents were starting to come, who survived this uprising, and taking the kids.
And that was pretty sad because other kids were really jealous and looking around, when the parents, one after one, were taking their kids.
This is still 1944 now?
That will be the beginning of '45.
And then on March 12, 1945, my Uncle Stach came.
That was terrible winter.
But this-- this is well known from '44 to '45.
It was a really strong winter.
Cold.
Cold.
Snow and cold.
And-- and he came-- and to take me.
He got a letter from my mother when she was letting him know where I am.
So he took me first to some kind of, you know, horses are taking you to-- they didn't have cars then.
[LAUGHS] And we were going to the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we went to hospital where my mother was, he took me to the-- to show me my mother in a casket.
She was dead.
She died of March 7, 1945.
And this uncle made her a stone on the place where she was buried, as [?
Yanina ?] [?
Zhaswavska, ?] died in March 7, 1945.
And he took me to his home.
That was-- the war ended in May.
So it was near-- they were Russian there in Warsaw, in every place.
And I was living in the house of my uncle, his mother, Maria, and their two kids, [?
Wanda ?] and [INAUDIBLE].
The boy, who was older, and a girl.
And started to go to school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And still as Bozenka.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I tried to be normal kid.
I mean, I knew my father is someplace, and I wrote a letter to him.
Don't know how he got it, but I have this letter, this line that I wrote, that my dear daddy, please come soon.
My mother died March 7, and she's buried in Skernivitz.
And-- come as soon as you can.
And the letter is dated September '45.
So at the end of '45, my father arrived from Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was pretty disappointing to me, because through all of these years, I imagined him to be a hero.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, Superman.
And he-- he was a-- a quite sad man who didn't talk too much and couldn't stand me talking.
And just asked me not to mention anything what I went through.
So he never knew any of these stories?
He didn't want to know the stories.
And he didn't want to go to-- to see where my mother was buried.
Maybe once.
And he--
Different name.
He never cared for, you know, things.
Also, he didn't have money.
He came right-- he was freed by Americans.
You know, this part of Germany, Murnau.
Zhaswavska. ?] Helena-- [?
Yanina ?] [?
Zhaswavska. ?]
Halina Majzel.
And I think, I assume that she took also, the priest came and give you-- you have to have certain testimony.
Testimony.
You talk to priest before you die in Catholic religion.
Rites.
Whatever.
In-- that was in-- in Polish-- Polish hospital.
And she was short time there.
They also shaved her head.
Because when I saw her in casket, she didn't have hair.
I remember she had green sweater.
It's terrible that-- or maybe it's healthy that I remember even details.
I quite remember well all this time when I was a kid.
And I see patients who completely for-- they were older than me, and they don't remember anything.
Well, first of all, I would-- I-- I was anti-Semitic.
[LAUGHS] And I didn't want to be Jewish.
And I didn't want to have anything to do with Jews.
And my father was giving me to different people, and someone advised him that there is a good orphanage home, children's home, for Jewish kids who were-- were survived the war.
And that was close to Warsaw, in Otvosk.
And he took me there and left me there.
And-- he didn't have a place to live.
He had to look for an apartment in Warsaw, which was really-- there were not too many houses which weren't destroyed, because it was bombed during uprising.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So my beginning to this children's home were that I really didn't want to talk to anybody.
And all kids were living their religion at school.
I went to school, and there were religion classes.
And I say, I am-- I am Catholic.
I will not leave.
I am not Jewish.
[LAUGHS] And but very soon, somehow, I became well adjusted to this children's home.
I met a lot of wonderful kids and people who are teachers, particularly the woman named Edda, which I really admired.
And she really spent with me day and night to teach me how to write and how to add, arithmetics.
They really were putting a lot of heart to these kids.
We were like brothers and sisters.
Yes.
At some point I became very close to Zionism.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt that the place for Jews is Israel.
That was when I was in children's home, when I made peace with other kids.
I was never-- they were also didn't teach us too much religion.
It was just that I felt if I am Jewish, I should be in Israel.
And I felt close to other kids, and they went to Israel.
But I couldn't, because my father was-- they escaped to Israel.
I never became [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] really interested in any Jewish.
Nobody really taught me Jewish religion.
OK?
[LAUGHS] It was just identity problem to-- to be Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we are already after the war.
Right.
No, I really told you.
It's nothing so-- so interesting as a child, really.
You know, I hardly could read, so it's nothing.
Like, you can see, I wrote Ola twice.
Yes.
Well, I just wrote, my dearest father, I'm so happy that I got information from-- about you.
I let you know that my mother died on March 7 in hospital in Skernivitz.
And she's buried in the cemetery on [?
Shelbiya ?].
Since March of 12, I am in Warsaw with my uncle.
And please, my-- father, I want you to come-- to come as soon as you can to Poland.
Kiss you.
Ola.
I wrote Ola twice.
And I have a lot of letters of my mother during this whole war, starting from '39, and the last one it's, I think, in '44.
And in all this time, when she writes about me, she writes, I'm sorry that Ola doesn't look like my mother.
Which means that I don't have blue eyes.
And there is always-- she would always swear to put Ola-- to find her a place because-- I don't know.
But it's strange that you know, you speak-- people are black and white in Los Angeles.
But when I was during the war in Poland, I was like black.
Everybody who looked at me knew that I'm Jewish.
[LAUGHS] It was just my eyes, I guess, mostly.
After the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish people considered-- well, they'd say, well, you look very interesting.
Maybe you-- you are like Greek or Italian or French.
So I said, no, I am Jewish.
Oh, no, no, no.
We didn't mean that.
Like it-- Jewish wouldn't be very-- a-- a compliment.
But Italian, it would be.
Just original, not like everybody else.
But my looks were-- were really bad, and that's what-- well, I-- I had a lot of guilt feeling about my mother.
